rulings.Case Title: LAURA ALBERT ET AL VS. INTERNATIONAL HIGH SCHOOL, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs' Notice And Motion For Order To Allow Plaintiffs' Designated Clinician To Attend Trevor Knoop'S Psychological Testing And Examinations
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 8, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF LAURA ALBERT's Motion For Order To Allow Plaintiffs' Designated Clinician To Attend Trevor Knoop'S Psychological Testing And Examinations Pro Tem Judge Adrienne Rogers, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is DENIED. As a general rule, observers are not allowed during mental examinations. (Golfland Entertainment Centers, Inc. v. Superior Court (2003) 108 Cal.App.4th 739, 747.) Trial courts, however, "retain the power" to order "prophylactic measures when needed." (Vinson v. Superior Court (1987) 43 Cal.3d 833, 846, italics added.) Plaintiffs have not established the presence of an unnamed "clinician or trusted person" is necessary. Plaintiffs' counsel expresses concern the tests administered by Drs. McDermott and Scott "may" injure Knoop. Jon Hershfield, MFT, opines he is "fearful" Knoop will be harmed during the examinations. This speculation does not satisfy plaintiffs' burden to produce credible and admissible evidence of a need for a third party to be present during the examinations. In contrast, defendants have offered evidence the presence of a third party may compromise the integrity of the examinations. (See Toyota Motor Sales, U.S.A., Inc. v. Superior Court (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 1391, 1397; Vinson v. Superior Court, supra, 43 Cal.3d at p. 844; Edwards v. Superior Court (1976) 13 Cal.3d 905, 911-912.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrienneleight@hotmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SCOTT J. MANDELL ET AL VS. GUERNEVILLE BUSINESS CORPORATION, INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses From Arthur O. Fonden And Scott J. Mandell
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 8, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANTS GUERNEVILLE BUSINESS CORPORATION, INC., AND RAY SHAHANI'S Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses From Arthur O. Fonden And Scott J. Mandell Pro Tem Judge Adrienne Rogers, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is DENIED. A motion to compel further responses to requests for admission, interrogatories, and requests for production must be accompanied by a separate statement. (California Rules of Court, rule 3.1345(a).) The motion was not supported by a separate statement, which is grounds to deny the motion. (Mills v. U.S. Bank (2008) 166 Cal.App.4th 871, 893.) The court declines to consider GBC's late-filed separate statement, filed after Fonden and Mandell opposed the motion to compel. GBC offers no justification for the late filing. (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 3.1300(d).) The court declines to impose monetary sanctions against GBC. (Code Civ. Proc., § 2023.010(h).) The record does not show a lack of substantial justification for the motion to compel and the court finds the imposition of sanctions would be unjust under the circumstances. (Code Civ. Proc., § 2023.030(a); Bunnell v. Superior Court of Alameda County (1967) 254 Cal.App.2d 720, 724.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrienneleight@hotmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY A BONNICI VS. CHARLES MCMACKIN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Charles Mcmackin, Carroll Henry Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint. (Tentative Ruling Pruposes Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, April 15, 2019, Line 5, Defendant Charles Mcmackin, Carroll Henry Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint. (Tentative Ruling Pruposes Only Part 2 Of 2) Finally, the fourth cause of action is also defective. "Conspiracy is not a cause of action . . . ." (Applied Equipment Corp. v. Litton Saudi Arabia Ltd. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 503, 510.) Plaintiff's allegation that Defendant Charles McMackin told him that he and Defendant Carroll Henry "agreed to share equally in the monetary damages Henry could recover in his related action" against Plaintiff (AC 26) does not plead an actionable claim. Plaintiff is granted a final opportunity to amend the first and second causes of action, if he can do so in good faith, to overcome the listed deficiencies. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY A BONNICI VS. CHARLES MCMACKIN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 8, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT CHARLES MCMACKIN, CARROLL HENRY DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2) Defendants Carroll Henry and Charles McMackin's demurrer to the amended complaint is sustained with leave to amend as to the first cause of action for defamation and the second cause of action for interference with economic relations, and without leave to amend as to the third and fourth causes of action. As to the first cause of action, "[t]he general rule is that the words constituting an alleged libel must be specifically identified, if not pleaded verbatim, in the complaint." (Kahn v. Bower (1991) 232 Cal.App.3d 1599, 1612 n.5; see also Okun v. Superior Court (1981) 29 Cal.3d 442, 458.) In order to plead a claim of defamation with sufficient certainty, Plaintiff must plead the substance of the alleged defamatory statement, to whom the statement was made, and that the statement was made within the one-year statute of limitations. The Court previously sustained Defendants' demurrer to Plaintiff's complaint on the ground that the allegations were conclusory and failed to provide sufficient information about the content of the alleged defamatory statements and none about the persons to whom such statements were made. The amended complaint does not cure those deficiencies. It merely repeats the prior vague allegations; the only change is a new allegation that the "rumors and innuendo, and outright lies" that Defendants allegedly have been spreading about the Plaintiff "include, but are not limited to, allegations of theft of properties offered for sale." (AC 6.) The amended complaint provides no information about the persons to whom the supposed defamatory statements were made, where they were made, or when they were made, other than the allegation that "the last known accusations against the plaintiff occurr[ed] over the weekend of March 24-25, 2018." (AC 12.) Similarly, the second cause of action for interference with economic relations fails to plead with sufficient certainty that Plaintiff had an economic relationship with a particular prospective buyer or seller, with the probability of future economic benefit to the Plaintiff, or Defendants' knowledge of the relationship, which are two of the essential elements of that claim. (See Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.3d 937, 944.) The third cause of action fails to state a cause of action for "elder abuse." The Elder Abuse Act makes certain enhanced remedies available to a plaintiff who proves abuse of an elder, i.e., a "person residing in this state, 65 years of age or older." (Welf. & Inst. Code § 15610.27.) However, the Act gives rise to a cause of action only if the plaintiff shows that the defendant is liable for physical abuse, neglect, or financial abuse of the elder, as those terms are defined in the Act. (Id. §§ 15657-15657.8.) Even if Section 15657.5 gives rise to an independent cause of action, Plaintiff has not pled any conduct that could constitute "financial abuse" of an elder within the meaning of the Act, which defines that term to mean taking, secreting, appropriating, obtaining, or retaining real or personal property of an elder for a wrongful use or with intent to defraud, or assisting in such conduct. (Id. § 15610.30(a)(1),(2); see Das v. Bank of America, NA (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 727, 743-745 ["The courts are divided over whether [Section 16567.5] and related provisions create independent causes of actions or merely enhance the remedies available under preexisting causes of action"; assuming arguendo that Section 15657.5 creates an independent cause of action, complaint failed to state claim against defendant bank for financial abuse of an elder].) (end of tenative ruling part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JAGPREET SUMMAN VS. PIER 39 STUDIO MANHATTAN, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, May 8, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT STUDIO SF ACCESSORIES, INC.'S Motion To Compel And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Adrienne Rogers, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion to compel further responses to General Form Interrogatories (Set 1), Form Interrogatories-Employment Law (Set 1), Requests For Production of Documents (Set 1), and Requests for Admission (Set 1). The motion is unopposed and is GRANTED. Plaintiff shall serve Code compliant responses, without objections, within 10 days of the date of this order. Defendant's motion for sanctions is DENIED. (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 8.1348(b); see also London v. Dri-Honing Corp. (2004) 117 Cal.App.4th 999, 1007.) "The discovery statutes evince an incremental approach to discovery sanctions, starting with monetary sanctions and ending with the ultimate sanction of termination." (Doppes v. Bentley Motors, Inc. (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 967, 992.) Defendant's request for monetary sanctions is not supported by a declaration "setting forth the facts supporting the amount of any monetary sanction sought." (Code Civ. Proc., § 2023.040.) Issue and evidentiary sanctions are unwarranted here, where defendant has not offered evidence plaintiff has failed to comply with a prior court order compelling discovery, nor has defendant demonstrated such an order would be futile. (See New Albertsons, Inc. v. Superior Court (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1403, 1428.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to adrienneleight@hotmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MCCOY LAW FIRM VS. ERIC STERLING ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 8, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT ERIC STERLING's MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND. Defendant Eric Sterling's motion to set aside default and default judgment is granted. While a proof of service by a registered process server creates a presumption of proper service, the court credits Mr. Sterling's uncontroverted declaration that he does not know the person named "Maria" with whom the summons was left, that he does not know a person with that name to be in charge of the hospital, and that he did not receive the summons and complaint. (Sterling Decl. 12, 13; see Fernandes v. Singh (2017) 16 Cal.App.5th 932, 941 ["declaration of non-service if credited by the trial court can rebut the presumption of proper service"].) Because there is no evidence showing that the person described on the proof of service and identified by first name only is a "person apparently in charge" of Mr. Sterling's place of business, as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 415.20(b), service was improper and the default judgment is vacated as void. (Bein v. Brechtel-Jochim Group, Inc. (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 1387, 1391; see Ellard v. Conway (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 540, 544 ["Under section 473, subdivision (d), the court may set aside a default judgment which is valid on its face, but void, as a matter of law, due to improper service."].) Alternatively, Defendant is entitled to have the default and default judgment set aside under Code of Civil Procedure section 473.5 because he did not receive actual notice in time to defend the action. (Sterling Decl. 6, 13; see Anastos v. Lee (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 1314, 1319 ["Discretionary relief based upon a lack of actual notice under section 473.5 empowers a court to grant relief from a default judgment where a valid service of summons has not resulted in actual notice to a party in time to defend the action."]; Tunis v. Barrow (1986) 184 Cal.App.3d 1069, 1077 ["Under section 473.5, . . . a defendant's right to relief from a default judgment initially turns on whether or not he had actual notice of the action in which the judgment was entered against him"].) Plaintiff McCoy Law Firm shall return to Plaintiff any property seized pursuant to the default judgment and shall release any judgment liens recorded. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SHANON NELSON VS. UNITED AIRLINES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 8, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT UNITED AIRLINES, INC.'s DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant United Airlines, Inc.'s demurrer to the first cause of action is sustained in part and overruled in part with leave to amend. That cause of action pleads that United was negligent by (1) damaging her custom built battery-powered wheelchair during the flight; (2) failing timely to provide a suitable loaner wheelchair; (3) failing to repair Plaintiff's wheelchair in a timely fashion; and (4) telling Plaintiff to "stop crying." (AC 25-28.) Because each theory of negligence alleges a distinct basis for liability, the Court will address them separately. (See Lilienthal & Fowler v. Sup. Crt. (1993) 12 Cal.App.4th 1848.) The Court's full tentative ruling is sent by email to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SHANON NELSON VS. UNITED AIRLINES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 8, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT UNITED AIRLINES, INC.'s MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant United Airlines, Inc.'s motion to strike is granted without leave to amend. As to the prayer for attorney's fees, Plaintiff concedes that there is no statutory, legal or contractual basis to recover the fees. The parties agree that punitive damages are not recoverable on the negligence claim, and the court has sustained without leave to amend Plaintiff's claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOHN MOORE VS. UNIVERSAL BEAUTY PRODUCTS INCORPORATED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And Consent Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 8, 2019, Line 10, PLAINTIFF JOHN MOORE's Motion To Approve Proposition 65 Settlement And Consent Judgment. Plaintiff John Moore's motion to approve Proposition 65 settlement is granted. No opposition filed and the settlement complies with the requirements for Proposition 65 settlements under Health & Safety Code section 25249.7. The Court finds that the injunctive relief that is required by the settlement providing that Defendant University Beauty Products Inc. shall only manufacture for sale, import for sale, sell or distribute for sale, in or into California, reformulated products complies with Health & Safety Code sections 25249.5-25249.14; the award of attorney's fees is reasonable under California law; and the penalty amount is reasonable based on the criteria set forth in Health & Safety Code section 25249.7(b)(2). The Court will sign the proposed order approving Proposition 65 settlement and consent judgment included with the moving papers. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: UUGANTSETSEG KHONGORZUL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, May 8, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to the third cause of action in plaintiff's complaint is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Plaintiff has leave to amend to clarify that Plaintiff is alleging a vicarious liability cause of action per Gov't Code § 815.2 and/or a direct claim per Gov't Code § 815 and Civil Code § 2100. A claim against a public entity must be pleaded with particularity, including the existence and source of a statutory duty. (Searcy v. Hemet Unified School Dist. (1986) 177 Cal.App.3d 792, 802.) Plaintiff can allege a vicarious liability claim based on conduct by City employees. Plaintiff alleges a sufficient "special relationship" to support the claim. (See C.A. v. William S. Hart Union High School Dist. (2012) 53 Cal.4th 861, 868-71, 877; Lopez v. Co. Cal. Rapid Transit (1985) 40 Cal.3d 780, 789 ["We believe these characteristics of public transportation, along with the duty of utmost care and diligence imposed by Civil Code section 2100, provide a more than ample basis for finding a special relationship between common carriers and their passengers."].) Plaintiff can allege a direct claim against the City pursuant to Lopez v. Co. Cal. Rapid Transit (1985) 40 Cal.3d 780, 785, fn. 2. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)
